posts.title: Django blog series (Part 1): Project setup and models
posts.excerpt: Start a Django blog from scratch by creating a project, configuring settings, and defining your first Post model.
posts.content:
## Overview
This is Part 1 of the **Django blog series**, where we set up the project and create our initial models.

### Creating the project
Run the following commands to start a new project and app:

```bash
django-admin startproject blogproject
cd blogproject
python manage.py startapp blog
```

### Defining the Post model
Here is a simple `Post` model with title, slug, and body fields:

```python
from django.db import models

class Post(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    slug = models.SlugField(unique=True)
    body = models.TextField()
    published_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.title
```

In later parts of the series we will wire this model up to views, templates, and add features like comments and pagination.

posts.title: Django blog series (Part 2): Views, URLs, and templates
posts.excerpt: Wire up your Django blog models to views, URL patterns, and templates to render your first list and detail pages.
posts.content:
## Overview
In Part 2 of the **Django blog series**, we build list and detail views and connect them to templates.

### List view
```python
from django.views.generic import ListView
from .models import Post

class PostListView(ListView):
    model = Post
    paginate_by = 5
    template_name = "blog/post_list.html"
```

### URL configuration
```python
from django.urls import path
from .views import PostListView

urlpatterns = [
    path("", PostListView.as_view(), name="post_list"),
]
```

With the URLs and basic templates in place, users can browse your blog posts.

posts.title: Django blog series (Part 3): Pagination, comments, and polish
posts.excerpt: Add pagination, a simple comment system, and some UI polish to complete the core of your Django blog.
posts.content:
## Overview
Part 3 of the **Django blog series** focuses on pagination, comments, and finishing touches.

### Adding comments
```python
class Comment(models.Model):
    post = models.ForeignKey(Post, related_name="comments", on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    name = models.CharField(max_length=80)
    email = models.EmailField()
    body = models.TextField()
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
```

### Implementing pagination
Use Django's `Paginator` or the generic `ListView` pagination support to split long lists of posts.

At the bottom of this tutorial you will find a comments section where readers can post questions or feedback about the series.

posts.title: Python testing deep dive (Part 1): Unit testing fundamentals
posts.excerpt: Learn the core concepts of unit testing in Python, including test discovery, assertions, and basic structure.
posts.content:
## Why unit tests?
Unit tests give you confidence that small pieces of your code behave as expected.

### A first unittest example
```python
import unittest

from mymath import add

class AddTests(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_adds_two_numbers(self):
        self.assertEqual(add(2, 3), 5)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
```

In this first part of the *Python testing deep dive* we cover how to organize tests and run them with `python -m unittest`.

posts.title: Python testing deep dive (Part 2): Fixtures, mocks, and parametrization
posts.excerpt: Go beyond the basics with reusable fixtures, mocking external services, and parametrized tests in Python.

posts.title: Modern React Hooks (Part 1): useState and useEffect basics
posts.excerpt: Get started with React Hooks by learning how useState and useEffect replace common class component patterns.

posts.title: Learn Python in 10 Minutes: Beginner Syntax Crash Course
posts.excerpt: A fast-paced introduction to Python syntax for complete beginners, designed to be read in about 10 minutes.
posts.content:
## Getting started
This short tutorial is aimed at beginners who want a quick overview of Python syntax.

### Hello, world
```python
print("Hello, world!")
```

We then cover variables, basic data types, and simple control flow, keeping each example small so you can finish in under 10 minutes.

posts.title: Python Lists and Dictionaries in 12 Minutes (Beginner)
posts.excerpt: Understand how to store and manipulate collections of data in Python using lists and dictionaries.
posts.content:
## Collections in Python
Lists and dictionaries are two of the most important built‑in data structures in Python.

### Lists
```python
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
numbers.append(4)
```

### Dictionaries
```python
user = {"name": "Ada", "age": 32}
print(user["name"])
```

By the end of this 12‑minute tutorial you will be comfortable using these structures in small scripts.

posts.excerpt: See how async/await builds on top of promises to make async code easier to read and maintain.

posts.title: Handling async errors and race conditions in 18 minutes
posts.excerpt: Learn practical patterns for dealing with async errors, timeouts, and race conditions in JavaScript.

posts.title: Binary Search in Arrays (Intermediate Data Structures)
posts.excerpt: Implement binary search iteratively and recursively, and understand its time complexity and edge cases.
posts.content:
## Problem
Given a sorted array and a target value, binary search finds the index of the target or reports that it is missing.

### Iterative implementation (Python)
```python
def binary_search(nums, target):
    left, right = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while left <= right:
        mid = (left + right) // 2
        if nums[mid] == target:
            return mid
        if nums[mid] < target:
            left = mid + 1
        else:
            right = mid - 1
    return -1
```

We then walk through the algorithm step by step and discuss common off‑by‑one mistakes.

posts.title: React Hooks patterns: useReducer and custom hooks in depth
posts.excerpt: Explore advanced patterns with React Hooks, including useReducer for complex state and reusable custom hooks.

posts.title: Practical unit testing in Python with unittest
posts.excerpt: Write your first real-world unit tests in Python using the built-in unittest framework.
posts.content:
## unittest in practice
In this tutorial we build a small module and cover it with tests using Python's standard `unittest` library.

### Example under test
```python
def normalize_username(name: str) -> str:
    return name.strip().lower().replace(" ", "_")
```

### Corresponding test
```python
import unittest
from users import normalize_username

class NormalizeUsernameTests(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_trims_and_lowercases(self):
        self.assertEqual(normalize_username("  Alice "), "alice")
```

By the end you will know how to run tests and interpret failures.

posts.title: Git branching for beginners: feature branches step by step
posts.excerpt: Learn a simple Git branching workflow using feature branches, from creating a branch to merging it back into main.
posts.content:
## Why branches?
Branches let you experiment with changes in isolation before merging them back into your main line of development.

### Creating a branch
```bash
git checkout -b feature/login-form
```

We then walk through committing, pushing, opening a pull request, and cleaning up merged branches.

posts.title: Advanced Git branching strategies for large teams
posts.excerpt: Compare GitFlow, trunk-based development, and release branching strategies and learn when to apply each.
posts.content:
## Scaling your branching model
As teams grow, choosing the right branching strategy becomes critical.

We discuss GitFlow, trunk‑based development, and hybrid models, including how to manage long‑lived release branches and hotfixes.

Real command examples illustrate how to keep history clean while enabling continuous delivery.

posts.title: Understanding Core Web Vitals in 2020
posts.excerpt: An introduction to Google's Core Web Vitals metrics and how they affect real user experience in 2020.
posts.content:
## Core Web Vitals
In 2020, Google introduced Core Web Vitals: LCP, FID, and CLS.

We explain what each metric measures, how to collect field data, and which tools you can use to debug issues.

Practical tips help you prioritize fixes that will have the biggest impact.

posts.title: Optimizing images for faster websites (2020 guide)
posts.excerpt: Learn how to choose formats, sizes, and loading strategies to make your site feel significantly faster.
posts.content:
## Why images matter
Images are often the largest contributor to page weight.

We cover choosing modern formats like WebP and AVIF, responsive images with `srcset`, and lazy‑loading techniques.

By applying these ideas you can improve both perceived and measured performance.

posts.title: Building an advanced REST API with Django and DRF
posts.excerpt: Use Django REST Framework to build a versioned, authenticated REST API with pagination and filtering.
posts.content:
## Project setup
In this tutorial we build an advanced REST API using Django and Django REST Framework.

### Example viewset
```python
from rest_framework import viewsets
from .models import Article
from .serializers import ArticleSerializer

class ArticleViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Article.objects.all()
    serializer_class = ArticleSerializer
```

We add authentication, rate limiting, and versioning, and discuss how to design resource URLs.

posts.title: Designing high-performance REST APIs in Go
posts.excerpt: Architect and implement a fast REST API in Go with proper routing, middleware, and concurrency patterns.
posts.content:
## Overview
Go is well suited to building fast network services.

### Simple handler example
```go
func hello(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    fmt.Fprintln(w, "Hello, API")
}
```

We then introduce routing with popular frameworks, middleware for logging and authentication, and strategies for safe concurrency.

    serializer_class = ArticleSerializer

comments.name: HooksLearner
comments.content: This deep dive into useReducer and custom hooks really helped me clean up a messy component tree. Thanks for the clear examples!

comments.name: FrontendDev
comments.content: I especially liked the explanation of when to choose useReducer over multiple useState calls. It finally clicked for me.

comments.name: TeamLead
comments.content: Shared this with my team and it sparked a great discussion about refactoring some of our older class components to use these patterns.

comments.name: JSNewbie
comments.content: The step-by-step counter example made hooks feel much less scary. Looking forward to the rest of the series!

comments.name: AlgoStudent
comments.content: The visual explanation of how left, right, and mid move each step really clarified why the loop condition is left <= right.
